_comment: v0.8 Tier-4 per-emulator base guides. gameId=null means 'standard setup for this emulator, accurate for any game'. The GuideResolver falls back to these when no game-specific (Tier 1-3) guide exists. Download URLs point at official source/release pages — never direct-to-APK mirrors, so links stay valid as projects publish new releases. dataAsOf is honest; the v0.6 'may be outdated' chip fires after 60 days.

steps.text: Download the latest Winlator Cmod APK and install it.

GPU driver: on a Snapdragon 8 Elite / Adreno 830, try the built-in Vortek (Universal) driver — Turnip 24.3.0 does not reliably support Adreno 830. On older Adreno 7xx, Turnip 24.3.0 is recommended instead. Install/select the driver in Settings → Graphics Drivers.
Create a new container (Containers tab → + ). These are recommended defaults for a high-end Adreno 830 / Snapdragon 8 Elite device — adjust to taste.

settings.DX level / mode: Auto / D3D11 (force D3D9 only for old games)
settings.Box64 / FEX preset: Performance (switch to Compatibility if a game won't start)
settings.GPU driver: Vortek (Universal) on Adreno 830; Turnip on Adreno 7xx
settings.Screen size / FPS: 1280x720 first; raise resolution only if it holds up

steps.text: Copy the game's folder (the one with the .exe) into the container's drive_c, e.g. Downloads, then point Winlator at the .exe.
Long-press the container → Run, browse to the game's .exe, and launch.
First launch is slow — DXVK compiles shaders. Let it sit a minute or two before deciding it froze.

GPU driver glossary: on Snapdragon 8 Elite / Adreno 830, use Vortek (Universal) — Turnip 24.3.0 is not reliable on Adreno 830. Turnip is the go-to on older Adreno 7xx. Vortek is built into Winlator 9+/GameNative; pick it from the Graphics Driver dropdown, no .zip needed.

DX wrapper glossary: DXVK translates DirectX 9/10/11 to Vulkan (fastest for most games); VKD3D handles DirectX 12; WineD3D maps DirectX to OpenGL (slower, but a useful fallback for stubborn old DX9 titles).
Box64 preset glossary: Performance runs games faster but can be less stable; Compatibility is slower but boots more games. Start on Performance and only drop to Compatibility if a game won't launch.
Env var glossary: DXVK_ASYNC=1 reduces shader-compile stutter. For very old games (pre-2003) that crash on modern GL/Vulkan extensions, try MESA_EXTENSION_MAX_YEAR=2003.

troubleshooting.symptom: Black screen on Adreno 830 / Snapdragon 8 Elite
troubleshooting.fix: Switch the GPU driver to Vortek (Universal) in the container settings — Turnip 24.3.0 does not reliably support Adreno 830 and is a common cause of black screens on this chip.
Switch DX wrapper to an older DXVK (2.3) or to WineD3D in the container's settings, then relaunch.
Set Box64 preset to Compatibility, and confirm the .exe is the actual game launcher, not a redistributable installer.
Lower the container screen size to 960x540, and make sure you selected the Turnip driver (not the system/llvmpipe one).
Install corefonts: container settings → Components → corefonts → install, then relaunch.

steps.text: Download the latest Winlator APK (brunodev85 build) and install it.
GPU driver: on a Snapdragon 8 Elite / Adreno 830, try Vortek (Universal) — Turnip is not reliable on Adreno 830. On older Adreno 7xx, install Turnip via Settings → Graphics Drivers.
Create a container tuned for older/indie titles. Recommended defaults for a high-end Adreno 830 device — adjust to taste.

settings.DX wrapper + version: DXVK 2.x (try WineD3D for old DX9 games)
settings.GPU driver: Vortek (Universal) on Adreno 830; Turnip on Adreno 7xx

steps.text: Put the game folder somewhere reachable and point the container at the .exe.
Run the container and browse to the game .exe.

GPU driver glossary: in GameNative, long-press the game → Container Settings → Graphics Driver → select Wrapper and turn ON 'Use Adrenotools Turnip', then pick a recent Turnip version (e.g. turnip25.3.0_R11 or turnip26.0.0_R8). If that's unstable, try Vortek (Universal) or SD 8 Elite from the same dropdown. To add a newer Turnip: app Settings → Driver Manager → Import ZIP from device.
DX wrapper glossary: DXVK = DirectX 9/10/11 → Vulkan (usually fastest); VKD3D = DirectX 12; WineD3D = DirectX → OpenGL (slower fallback, sometimes smoother for old DX9 games).
Box64 glossary: Performance = faster, Compatibility = boots more games. Env var MESA_EXTENSION_MAX_YEAR=2003 helps very old games run on modern drivers.

troubleshooting.symptom: Black screen on Adreno 830 / Snapdragon 8 Elite
troubleshooting.fix: Switch the GPU driver to Vortek (Universal) — Turnip does not reliably support Adreno 830.
Try WineD3D instead of DXVK — DX9 titles sometimes run smoother on it.
Switch Box64 preset to Stability/Compatibility and retry.

steps.text: Install a CURRENT GameNative build from the official releases page — the latest is v1.0.0-prerelease (~May 30, 2026). Pick the Modern APK on Android 11+ (the Legacy APK covers Android 8–14). A recent build matters: the per-game Import Config feature only exists on newer builds, so don't run an old APK.

GPU driver on Snapdragon 8 Elite / Adreno 830 is NOT a settled single winner as of 2026-06 — treat it as two options to TEST per game. Option A: a patched AdrenoTools Turnip from the Mesa 26.1.x line (the Winlator v11 stable / ~Apr 2026 build shipped Mesa Turnip 26.1.0 with whitebelyash Adreno-8-series patches) — load it via graphics driver = wrapper with the AdrenoTools Turnip flag. Option B: Vortek as a fallback when a game won't render on Turnip. Older guidance pinning 'Turnip 24.3.0' or saying 'never Turnip on 830, always Vortek' is outdated; test both and keep whichever boots and runs the game.

Create a container. The values below reflect a real current GameNative stack exported from an Adreno 830 / Snapdragon 8 Elite device (as of 2026-06). 'recommended' = sane starting point; 'try' = test per game, no single value wins everywhere.

settings.WoW64 mode: On (recommended; runs 32-bit without a separate WoW64 prefix)
settings.Emulator (x86 translation): FEXCore, current build (recommended) — try Box64 0.3.7 as an alternative, especially for DX9-era titles
settings.Graphics driver: wrapper with the AdrenoTools Turnip flag (patched Mesa Turnip 26.1.x); try Vortek if a game won't render
settings.DX wrapper: DXVK for DX9/10/11; VKD3D for DX12 (try WineD3D / renderer=gl for stubborn DX9-era games)
settings.Dependencies: d3dcompiler_47, vcrun2022 (install via Components if a game asks)
settings.Resolution / FPS: Native; lower the in-game scale if it stutters

steps.text: Copy your game files into GameNative's storage, then add the game. Each game also gets a .gamenative JSON config written into its own root folder, which is what the Import Config flow reads/writes.

Import a per-game config (current builds): press and hold the game in your library → 3-dots menu → Import Config, then point it at a .gamenative file. This is the config-import/export feature added in PR #232; if you don't see the Import button, your build is too old — update from the releases page. The imported settings land in that game's .gamenative file in its root folder.
GameNative is a strong pick for Unreal Engine 4/5 and DX12 titles via Proton + VKD3D. Modern AAA still won't reliably hit 60 — 'playable' is the realistic target.

DX-era nuance: FEXCore is the recommended translator for most modern titles, but some DX9-era games run better on Box64 0.3.7 + WineD3D with renderer=gl. Treat this as a per-game thing to TRY, not a rule.

Driver glossary (2026-06): on Adreno 830 there are two real options to test — (A) patched AdrenoTools Turnip from the Mesa 26.1.x line (graphics driver = wrapper + Turnip flag), or (B) Vortek as a fallback. Neither is a guaranteed winner; pick per game. DXVK = DX9/10/11 → Vulkan, VKD3D = DX12 → Vulkan, WineD3D = DX → OpenGL fallback.

troubleshooting.fix: Swap the driver and re-test: if you were on the patched Turnip (Mesa 26.1.x wrapper) try Vortek, or vice-versa. Then try Box64 0.3.7 instead of FEXCore (helps some DX9-era titles). Finally confirm the game's install path/drive is correct.
Confirm VKD3D is on; if it still crashes, try a DX11 launch option if the game offers one, and re-test on the other GPU driver (Turnip patched ↔ Vortek).
Your build predates the config import/export feature (PR #232). Update to a current GameNative build from the releases page, then retry press-and-hold game → 3 dots → Import Config.
Disable the Steam overlay in the game's launch options; some DRM needs an initial online activation before offline play works.
Keep DXVK async on, lower the in-game resolution scale, and close background apps to free RAM.

steps.text: Download the latest GameHub APK and install it.
GPU driver: on a Snapdragon 8 Elite / Adreno 830, try Vortek (Universal) — Turnip is not reliable on Adreno 830. On older Adreno 7xx, install Turnip via GameHub's driver section.
Create a container. GameHub simplifies container management vs vanilla Winlator — defaults are sensible. Recommended values for a high-end Adreno 830 device.

settings.GPU driver: Vortek (Universal) on Adreno 830; Turnip on Adreno 7xx

steps.text: Copy the game folder in, then point GameHub at the .exe.
Run the container and select the game .exe.

GPU driver glossary: in GameNative, Graphics Driver → Wrapper + 'Use Adrenotools Turnip' (recent version) is the primary pick for Adreno 830; Vortek (Universal) or SD 8 Elite are fallbacks in the same dropdown.
DX wrapper glossary: DXVK = DX9/10/11 → Vulkan; VKD3D = DX12; WineD3D = DX → OpenGL fallback. Box64 Performance = faster, Compatibility = boots more games.

troubleshooting.symptom: Black screen on Adreno 830 / Snapdragon 8 Elite
troubleshooting.fix: Switch the GPU driver to Vortek (Universal) — Turnip does not reliably support Adreno 830.
Switch DX wrapper to DXVK 2.3 or WineD3D, then relaunch.
Verify the GPU driver is selected (Vortek on Adreno 830, Turnip on Adreno 7xx), not the system driver, and try the Compatibility Box64 preset.

steps.text: Download Mobox (micewine) and install it.
Mobox uses Box86/Box64 + Wine. For older/OpenGL games, leave DXVK off — Box64 + WineD3D is lighter. Recommended values for a high-end Adreno 830 device.

settings.DX wrapper + version: WineD3D (no DXVK) for old games; DXVK if available for DX11
settings.DX level / mode: D3D9 / OpenGL path for old titles
settings.GPU driver: Stock Adreno; on Adreno 830 use Vortek (Universal) if your Mobox build offers it — avoid Turnip on Adreno 830

steps.text: Copy your game into Mobox's working directory and point it at the .exe.
Launch the game from Mobox.
Mobox shines on older DX9 / OpenGL / 2D games where Cmod's DXVK overhead is wasted. For modern DX11/12 AAA, use GameNative or Cmod instead.
GPU driver glossary: on Adreno 830, Wrapper + 'Use Adrenotools Turnip' (a recent Turnip build) is the primary choice; Vortek (Universal) and SD 8 Elite are fallbacks in the Graphics Driver dropdown.
DX wrapper glossary: WineD3D = DirectX → OpenGL (lightest, good for old games); DXVK = DX9/10/11 → Vulkan; VKD3D = DX12. Box64 Performance = faster, Compatibility = boots more games.

troubleshooting.symptom: Black screen on Adreno 830 / Snapdragon 8 Elite
troubleshooting.fix: Switch the GPU driver to Vortek (Universal) if your Mobox build provides it — Turnip does not reliably support Adreno 830.
Mobox is weakest on modern DirectX — enable DXVK if available, or switch to Winlator Cmod / GameNative for that title.
Confirm Box64 dynarec is on; lower the resolution; close other apps.

steps.text: Download the patched NetherSX2 APK and install it.
PS2 BIOS required (e.g. SCPH-70012.bin or your regional equivalent). Place it in the bios folder. This comes from a PS2 you own — there's no download here; place it once you have it.
On first launch, point NetherSX2 at your BIOS folder when prompted, then at your games folder.
Recommended settings for Snapdragon 8-class devices.
Put your PS2 games (.iso / .chd) in a folder and add it as a game directory.
.chd is smaller than .iso and runs identically — convert large ISOs to save space.

troubleshooting.fix: Wrong/missing BIOS, or the ISO is bad. Re-dump the BIOS and verify the game dump.
Drop internal resolution to 1x-2x, ensure Vulkan renderer, and keep speed hacks enabled.

troubleshooting.symptom: Graphical glitches on a specific game
troubleshooting.fix: Check the per-game guide (some PS2 titles need a specific blending/half-pixel-offset setting).

steps.text: Download ARMSX2 and install it.
PS2 BIOS required. Place it in the bios folder. This comes from a PS2 you own — there's no download here; place it once you have it.
Use the Vulkan renderer at a conservative resolution first.
Point the emulator at your BIOS and games folder, then launch a title.

troubleshooting.fix: ARMSX2 is newer/less mature than NetherSX2 — if a game misbehaves, try NetherSX2-patch for that title.

steps.text: Install DuckStation from the Play Store or its official site.
PS1 BIOS recommended (scph5501.bin / scph1001.bin). Place it in the bios folder. DuckStation can run without it but BIOS improves accuracy.
PS1 is trivial for modern hardware — crank it.
Add your PS1 games (.chd / .bin+.cue / .pbp).
Pick the game from the list and play.

troubleshooting.fix: Enable PGXP (geometry correction) in settings — it's the PS1 signature fix.
Use an .m3u playlist file listing each disc so in-game disc swaps work.

steps.text: Install PPSSPP (free) or PPSSPP Gold from the Play Store or official site.
No BIOS needed. Recommended settings for high-end devices.
Put your PSP games (.iso / .cso) anywhere and browse to them in PPSSPP.
Select the game and play.
PSP runs essentially perfectly on Snapdragon 8-class hardware. If a game stutters, switch backend to Vulkan and disable frameskip.

troubleshooting.symptom: Black screen on a specific game
troubleshooting.fix: Try the OpenGL backend for that title — a few PSP games render better on GL than Vulkan.
Raise the audio latency setting slightly in System settings.

steps.text: Download the latest Eden APK from its official release page and install it.
Switch keys (prod.keys) and firmware are required. These come from a Nintendo Switch you own — there's no download for them. Import them via Eden's key/firmware installer once you have them.
Install the recommended Turnip driver — Switch emulation is very GPU-driver-sensitive.
Recommended settings for a high-end Snapdragon / Gen 2.

settings.Resolution: 1x (docked) — raise only if it holds up

steps.text: Add your dumped games (.nsp / .xci) to a folder and set it as the games directory.
Install firmware + keys, add your game, and launch.
First launch of any Switch game stutters while shaders build. It smooths out on the second run from the disk cache. 30-40 fps is a good result for demanding first-party titles.

troubleshooting.fix: Re-dump prod.keys from your console and reinstall current firmware — mismatched/old keys are the #1 cause.
Enable async shaders + disk pipeline cache, play the same area twice to build the cache, and make sure Turnip (not the system driver) is selected.
Lower resolution to 1x, disable any speed hacks, and confirm you have enough free RAM.

steps.text: Download aPS3e and install it.
PS3 firmware required (PS3UPDAT.PUP). Install via the emulator's firmware installer. This comes from official Sony firmware — there's no download here; install it once you have it.
Install the recommended Turnip driver — PS3 emulation is extremely GPU-heavy.
Set expectations low — PS3 on mobile is bleeding-edge.

settings.Write color buffers: Only if a game needs it

steps.text: Install your PS3 game (decrypted, from a console you own) into the emulator's game folder.
PS3 is the hardest console to emulate on Android. Even on a high-end Snapdragon, most games are 15-30 fps and many don't boot. Treat any playable result as a bonus.

troubleshooting.fix: Many PS3 games simply don't run yet. Check the per-game guide / community reports before assuming your setup is wrong.
Keep resolution at 1x, use the SPU recompiler, and accept that demanding titles are not yet viable.

steps.text: Install Dolphin from its official site (or the Play Store beta).
No BIOS needed. Recommended settings for high-end devices.
Add your GameCube/Wii games (.rvz / .iso). RVZ is the compressed modern format.
Pick the game and play. For Wii motion games, configure motion/IR controls first.
Enable Hybrid Ubershaders to avoid the classic Dolphin shader-compilation stutter on first play.

troubleshooting.fix: Set Shader Compilation to Hybrid Ubershaders in Graphics → Advanced.
Map the Wii Remote to touch/gyro in the controller settings before launching.

steps.text: Download Azahar (the maintained Citra successor) and install it.
No BIOS strictly required. Recommended settings.
Add your 3DS dumps (.3ds / .cia / .cxi).
Select the game and play. Configure the dual-screen layout to taste.
3DS runs great on modern hardware. Pick a screen layout (top/bottom, side-by-side) that suits the game.

troubleshooting.fix: Some 3DS titles want shared-system-data dumped from a real 3DS — see the per-game guide.

steps.text: Install Mupen64Plus FZ from the Play Store or its release page.
No BIOS needed. Recommended settings.
Add your N64 ROMs (.z64 / .n64 / .v64).
Select the game and play.
GLideN64 is the most accurate plugin. A few N64 games need a specific per-game profile, which Mupen usually applies automatically.

troubleshooting.symptom: Graphical glitches in a specific game
troubleshooting.fix: Open that game's profile and try toggling the 'native res factor' or a game-specific GLideN64 hack.

steps.text: Download Vita3K for Android and install it.
No BIOS, but you need your own dumped games. Install the PSVita firmware components Vita3K asks for on first run.
Install your dumped Vita games (.vpk or extracted) via Vita3K's installer.
Launch the title from the Vita3K library.
Vita emulation is still maturing — compatibility varies a lot by game. Check community reports before expecting a given title to be playable.

troubleshooting.fix: Confirm the dump is complete and from a console you own; many Vita titles are still partially supported.

steps.text: Download the Android Cemu build and install it.
No BIOS, but games must be your own dumps. Keys (from your console) are needed to decrypt some formats.
Install the recommended Turnip driver.
Recommended settings.
Add your dumped Wii U games and point Cemu at the folder.
Android Cemu is experimental. Big first-party titles may run at 30-50 fps on a high-end Snapdragon; expect rough edges.

troubleshooting.fix: Enable async shader compile and replay areas to build the cache.

steps.text: Install RetroArch from the Play Store or retroarch.com.
In RetroArch, go to Online Updater → Core Downloader and install the core for your system (e.g. Snes9x for SNES, mGBA for GBA, Beetle PSX for PS1).
Some cores need BIOS files (PS1, Saturn, etc.). Place them in the System folder. These come from hardware you own — there's no download here; place them once you have them.
Put your ROMs in a folder and Load Content to play, or scan the folder to build a playlist.
RetroArch is a multi-system front-end. Pick the recommended core per system — the default isn't always the most accurate.

troubleshooting.fix: You probably installed the wrong core for the ROM type, or a required BIOS is missing from the System folder.
Enable Run-Ahead (1 frame) in Latency settings for a snappier feel.
